script:
Hello, I am Josh Weinberg and today is Saturday, June 27, 2026 and The Portfolio Podcast QQQM update is as of June 27 2026 at 12:42 AM ET. The portfolio is down one point six one percent on the session, a loss of one thousand six hundred and six dollars and forty-eight cents, bringing the current value to ninety-eight thousand three hundred and ninety-three dollars and fifty-two cents against a one hundred thousand dollar base. A rough close to the week.

Apple is lobbying the Trump administration for clearance to purchase memory chips from ChangXin Memory Technologies — a Chinese firm sitting on the Pentagon's blacklist. That is not a minor compliance footnote. That is the world's most valuable company asking the White House for an exemption to buy from a sanctioned supplier, and the market noticed.

Tech stocks led the broad market lower on Friday. Investors rotated out of AI and growth names, with the Nasdaq and S&P 500 both finishing the week in the red. The Dow Jones, by contrast, closed its third consecutive week higher, lifted by strength in pharmaceuticals, materials, and basic resources, aided in part by rising gold prices. Oil prices fell on the session as shipping traffic resumed through the Strait of Hormuz following earlier geopolitical tension, giving some relief to the broader economy but not enough to rescue tech-heavy indices from a week defined by selling pressure.

The top story today is Apple, and it carries weight far beyond one trading session. According to a report from the Financial Times, published late Friday, Apple is actively lobbying the White House for approval to source memory chips from ChangXin Memory Technologies, known as CXMT, a Chinese semiconductor company the Pentagon has placed on its entity blacklist. The reason Apple is pushing for this is straightforward: rising memory chip prices are creating meaningful financial pressure on the company, and CXMT represents a lower-cost alternative to existing suppliers. Apple has not commented. Neither has the White House. Neither has CXMT. That silence matters. When a company the size of Apple goes to the executive branch for a national security carve-out, the political and regulatory risk is substantial. If approved, it signals a softening of chip trade restrictions that could benefit other tech names in this portfolio. If denied, Apple faces continued cost pressure heading into the iPhone 18 cycle. The bearish sentiment score on this headline came in at zero point seven zero — one of the stronger negative readings we track. Apple's weighting in QQQM sits at eight point nine seven percent, the largest single position in the fund. This is the story to watch as the weekend develops. The Financial Times report, sourced to unnamed parties, has not been confirmed by any of the named parties. That uncertainty alone is worth pricing in.

Turning to Microsoft, which checks in as the second largest holding at eight point four two percent of the portfolio. There were three meaningful headlines crossing Friday evening. First, Bernstein released an analysis of first quarter twenty twenty-six hyperscaler performance, and the finding is notable for what it says about the competitive landscape. According to Bernstein, Google Cloud has emerged as the leading AI cloud platform after a strong first quarter, with accelerating cloud growth and rising profitability. That framing puts Microsoft Azure in a direct competitive context, and not a favorable one in terms of relative momentum. Second, and more constructively for Microsoft, a separate report from Wedbush Securities analyst Dan Ives characterized the current environment as a, quote, Twilight Zone market — a phrase Ives used specifically to describe a period of dislocation for quality tech names including Microsoft, Nvidia, Meta, and others. Ives flagged these as investor opportunities within the dissonance. Third, Microsoft stock reportedly rose on Friday, with coverage framing it as a question of whether the AI software trade is set for a comeback. Watch Azure's next growth print and any commentary on Copilot adoption rates as the primary catalysts for Microsoft's next directional move.

Meanwhile, Nvidia is drawing significant analyst attention after its market capitalization fell below five trillion dollars. Two separate pieces published late Friday framed the pullback as a potential buying opportunity, with one headline specifically calling it, quote, not too late to get in on this market beater. A second piece grouped Nvidia among beaten-down AI chip stocks worth considering in the sell-off, noting that sector-wide selling often drags quality names down alongside weaker ones. Nvidia's QQQM weight stands at eight point one five percent, making it the third largest position. One note worth flagging: a headline about SpaceX's stock volatility appeared in Nvidia's news feed — that is noise, not signal, and has no direct bearing on Nvidia's fundamentals. The core story here is the sub-five-trillion market cap level and whether institutional buyers step in at current levels. Chip export policy remains the long-tail risk.

Over at Amazon, weighted at five point five two percent of the portfolio, the news cycle was quiet on the company directly. One tangentially related headline covered Kroger's new CEO and a grocery turnaround strategy, which surfaced in Amazon's news feed given the competitive overlap in grocery retail. No direct Amazon-specific news crossed on Friday. A quiet session for the stock, though the broader tech selloff likely created headwinds across the consumer discretionary space. Watch for any AWS commentary or advertising revenue data points in the coming weeks as the next fundamental catalyst.

Broadcom sits at four point six one percent of the portfolio and had no material company-specific headlines on Friday. In a week defined by AI chip volatility and cloud competition narratives, Broadcom's custom ASIC business and its positioning with hyperscaler clients remains a background theme worth monitoring. The stock trades in close correlation to broader semiconductor sentiment, which was negative on the session.

Meta Platforms, at four point four eight percent, was specifically named in the Wedbush note from Dan Ives alongside Microsoft, Nvidia, and others as a quality name caught in the current market dislocation. No earnings or product-specific news crossed Friday. Meta's advertising revenue trajectory and its AI infrastructure spending remain the two variables that will drive the next meaningful move. The Twilight Zone framing from Ives suggests analysts see the current weakness as a setup rather than a structural breakdown.

Alphabet carries a combined weight of just over eight percent across its two share classes, GOOGL at four point one two percent and GOOG at three point eight nine percent. The Bernstein hyperscaler analysis is the dominant Alphabet story of the day, and it is a constructive one. Google Cloud leading in incremental AI cloud revenue in the first quarter of twenty twenty-six is a specific, data-driven finding — not analyst opinion dressed up as fact. Accelerating cloud growth combined with rising profitability is the combination that institutional investors track most closely. GOOG had no individual news today, a quiet session for the Class C shares specifically, with no price-specific data available to report.

Tesla rounds out the top ten at three point five four percent of the portfolio. No Tesla-specific headlines crossed in Friday's cycle. The stock remains sensitive to broader risk-off sentiment in consumer discretionary, and the ongoing AI and EV market narrative continues to shape its trading range. No new product or regulatory developments to report.

Costco, the only Consumer Staples name in the top ten at two point eight one percent, had no news today. Costco typically trades as a defensive holding relative to the tech-heavy remainder of this portfolio, and in weeks where growth names sell off, the relative performance dynamic is worth watching.

Three things to watch in the next forty-eight hours. First, any official response from the White House, Apple, or CXMT regarding the chip exemption request reported by the Financial Times — timing unknown but this is a weekend story with Monday market implications. Second, watch for any follow-up analyst notes on the Bernstein hyperscaler Q1 rankings, particularly any response from Microsoft Azure bulls defending their cloud growth thesis. Third, monitor semiconductor export policy developments over the weekend — any executive action or agency guidance touching chip trade restrictions would directly affect Nvidia, Broadcom, and Apple simultaneously.

That is your Saturday morning portfolio update. The next episode drops at five PM Eastern Time on the next trading day. Overnight, keep your eye on any official statement out of the White House regarding Apple's CXMT exemption request — that story has the potential to set the tone for the entire tech complex when markets reopen. I am Josh Weinberg. Stay disciplined.
